name: Quadratic Equations
whatIsIt: A QUADRATIC equation has an x² term, in the form ax² + bx + c = 0. It usually has TWO solutions (roots). One neat way to solve is by FACTORISING into two brackets: x² + 5x + 6 = (x + 2)(x + 3) = 0, so x = −2 or x = −3 (because if a product is zero, one factor must be zero). Simple ones like x² = 9 solve by taking square roots (x = 3 or −3).
whyNeeded: Quadratics describe curved paths, areas, and optimisation — a thrown ball's flight, the largest area for a fence, and countless physics and design problems. They're a milestone of secondary algebra.
realLifeUses: The path of a thrown ball (a parabola)
Finding dimensions when only the area is known
Largest area for a fixed perimeter
Break-even and profit problems in business

vocabulary.meaning: An equation with an x² term: ax² + bx + c = 0.
A value of x that makes the equation true.
If a product = 0, at least one factor = 0.

story.title: Two Answers
story.text: Sir writes x² + 5x + 6 = 0. 'One answer or two?' Tara factorises: she needs two numbers that multiply to 6 and add to 5 — that's 2 and 3. So (x + 2)(x + 3) = 0. 'Either bracket can be zero,' she says, 'so x = −2 OR x = −3.' Sir nods: 'Quadratics usually give two roots.'

visual.caption: The parabola y = x² − 5x + 6 crosses the x-axis at x = 2 and x = 3 — exactly the two solutions (roots) of the quadratic.

teachingGallery.title: Factor pairs and roots
teachingGallery.note: Find two numbers that multiply to c and add to b.

standardMethod.summary: Write it as ax² + bx + c = 0; factorise into two brackets (or take roots for x² = k); set each factor to zero to get the roots.
steps: Arrange to the form ax² + bx + c = 0 (one side zero).
For x² = k, take square roots: x = +√k or −√k.
To factorise x² + bx + c: find two numbers that MULTIPLY to c and ADD to b.
Set each bracket to zero (zero-product rule) and solve for the two roots. Check each in the equation.
Two numbers multiplying to 6, adding to 5: 2 and 3.
Take square roots of both sides.
x = 4 or x = −4.

commonMistakes.mistake: Giving only one solution when a quadratic usually has two.
commonMistakes.fix: Set EACH factor to zero — most quadratics have TWO roots. For x² = 9, both 3 and −3 work.

commonMistakes.mistake: Choosing numbers that multiply right but don't add to b (or vice versa).
commonMistakes.fix: The pair must BOTH multiply to c AND add to b. Check both conditions before factoring.

challenge.q: Ravi solves x² = 9 and writes x = 3 only. What's the fix?

options.label: Also x = −3 — quadratics have two roots

challenge.answer: Also x = −3 — quadratics have two roots

questions.q: If a product of two brackets = 0, then…

teachBackPrompt: Show a grown-up how x² + 5x + 6 = 0 becomes (x+2)(x+3)=0 by finding two numbers that multiply to 6 and add to 5, and explain why that gives TWO answers, x = −2 and x = −3.

revisionCard.summary: Quadratic: ax² + bx + c = 0, usually TWO roots. x² = k → x = ±√k. Factorise x² + bx + c by finding two numbers that MULTIPLY to c and ADD to b; set each bracket = 0 (zero-product rule). Always check both roots.

vedicMethod.name: Vedic: Completing the Square (Puranapuranabhyam)
vedicMethod.whenToUse: To solve a quadratic by 'completing' it into a perfect square.
steps: For x² + 6x + 5 = 0, take half the middle number: 6 ÷ 2 = 3.
Complete the square: x² + 6x + 9 = (x + 3)². We added 9, so 5 becomes 5, and (x+3)² = 9 − 5 = 4.
Take roots: x + 3 = ±2.

alternateMethods.name: Vedic: roots as average ± gap (Vyashtisamashti)
alternateMethods.whenToUse: When the quadratic is x² − (sum)x + (product) and you can guess two numbers.
steps: For x² − 10x + 21: the roots add to 10, so their average is 10 ÷ 2 = 5.
The roots are 5 − g and 5 + g for some gap g.
Their product is 21: (5−g)(5+g) = 25 − g² = 21 → g² = 4 → g = 2.
Roots: 5 − 2 = 3 and 5 + 2 = 7.

alternateMethods.name: Vedic: difference of roots (Chalana-Kalanabhyam)
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To get how far apart the two roots are, straight from the numbers.
steps: For ax² + bx + c, the gap between the roots is √(b² − 4ac) ÷ a.
Difference of roots = √1 = 1.
Indeed the roots are 3 and 4, which differ by 1.

rememberIt.unpack: For a x^2 + b x + c = 0. The b^2 - 4ac part (the discriminant) tells you the number of real answers. Tip: sing it to 'Pop Goes the Weasel'.

studentQuestions.q: When do I use the formula instead of factorising?
studentQuestions.a: Try factorising first - it is fast when it works. If the equation will not factor into neat whole numbers, the formula ALWAYS works.
The discriminant: positive -> two real answers, zero -> one repeated answer, negative -> no real answers.

formulas.name: Quadratic formula
formulas.remember: Sing it to 'Pop Goes the Weasel'
formulas.whenToUse: Solve a x^2 + b x + c = 0 that will not factor nicely.

formulas.name: Discriminant
formulas.remember: The sign of D = how many real roots
